news_summary: NASA advances lunar exploration with Artemis II success, extends Voyager 1's mission by conserving its instruments, and discovers an unexpected atmosphere on a distant frozen world.
news_summary_fr: La NASA fait progresser l'exploration lunaire grâce au succès d'Artemis II, prolonge la mission de Voyager 1 en préservant ses instruments et découvre une atmosphère inattendue sur un monde gelé lointain.
news_summary_es: La NASA avanza en la exploración lunar gracias al éxito de Artemis II, prolonga la misión de la Voyager 1 conservando sus instrumentos y descubre una atmósfera inesperada en un lejano mundo helado.

articles.title: NASA just took a huge step toward the Moon after Artemis II success
articles.summary: Artemis II proved NASA’s deep space systems are ready for the next leap. Orion survived its high-speed return with improved heat shield performance and pinpoint landing accuracy, while the SLS rocket nailed its trajectory. Even the launch pad upgrades paid off, with minimal damage despite the powerful liftoff. With only minor issues to resolve, NASA is now gearing up for Artemis III and future Moon missions.
articles.content:
After NASA's Artemis II mission ended with a successful splashdown, engineers began closely reviewing data from across the mission. Their goal is to evaluate how major systems performed, including the Orion spacecraft, the SLS (Space Launch System) rocket, and ground infrastructure at Kennedy Space Center in Florida. Early findings suggest the test flight achieved its objectives and helped open the door to future missions, including Artemis III, long-term lunar exploration, and eventual human missions to Mars.

Orion completed a 694,481-mile journey around the Moon before reentering Earth's atmosphere and landing in the Pacific Ocean off San Diego on April 10. During reentry, the spacecraft traveled at nearly 35 times the speed of sound, relying on its thermal protection system to shield both the crew and vehicle.

Initial inspections indicate the heat shield performed as expected, with no unusual issues observed. Images captured by divers shortly after splashdown, along with follow-up checks aboard the recovery ship, show that the charring seen during Artemis I was significantly reduced in both size and amount. The results also match what engineers predicted based on ground testing in arc jet facilities.

Additional imagery taken from aircraft during reentry will be analyzed in the coming weeks. This data will help pinpoint when minor surface changes occurred and provide deeper insight into how the heat shield behaved under extreme conditions.

Post-Flight Inspections and Heat Shield Analysis

The crew module is scheduled to return to NASA Kennedy this month for further evaluation during de-servicing at the Multi-Payload Processing Facility. Engineers will carry out detailed inspections, collect flight data, remove reusable components such as avionics, and address any remaining hazards like excess fuel or coolant.

Later this summer, the heat shield will be transported to NASA's Marshall Space Flight Center in Huntsville, Alabama. There, teams will extract samples and conduct internal X-ray scans to better understand how the materials responded during reentry.

The ceramic tiles on the spacecraft's upper backshell also met expectations. Reflective thermal tape, which is designed to burn away during reentry, remains visible in several areas. This tape helps regulate temperatures while in space but does not play a role in protecting the spacecraft during atmospheric entry.

Orion's landing was highly accurate, touching down just 2.9 miles from its target. Early analysis shows its entry speed was within one mile per hour of predicted values.

Recovered Components and Ongoing Investigations

Following splashdown, teams in San Diego removed several components for inspection and reuse before sending the spacecraft back to Kennedy. These included seats, video processing units, camera controllers, storage equipment, and suit umbilicals from the Orion Crew Survival System.

Engineers are also examining hardware related to a urine vent line issue that occurred during the mission. The team is collecting data to determine the cause and develop fixes ahead of Artemis III.

SLS Rocket Performance Meets Mission Goals

The SLS rocket that carried Artemis II into space also performed well. Early assessments show it successfully delivered Orion to its planned trajectory. At main engine cutoff, when the RS-25 engines shut down, the spacecraft was traveling at more than 18,000 miles per hour and reached its intended orbital insertion point with high precision.

Launch Pad and Ground Systems Hold Up Under Stress

After launch, engineers carried out a thorough review of the launch pad and mobile launcher systems. Improvements made after Artemis I, including reinforcements and design adjustments, proved effective. The structures experienced minimal damage despite the intense forces generated during liftoff.

Teams cleaned and inspected the systems immediately after launch. Some components had been strengthened, such as elevator doors, while others were designed to flex under pressure, including gaseous distribution panels. Protective barriers were also added in key areas. These changes allowed systems that handle air, gas, cooling, and water flow to remain operational after launch.

The mobile launcher has since been moved back to the Vehicle Assembly Building at Kennedy Space Center, where it will undergo repairs and preparations for future missions.

Recovery Operations and Next Steps for Artemis Program

Recovery teams, working alongside military partners, safely retrieved the crew and spacecraft. Navy divers assisted each crew member out of Orion and brought them aboard USS John P. Murtha. The spacecraft was then recovered and returned to Naval Base San Diego.

Data from Artemis II, the first crewed mission in the Artemis program, is now being used to prepare for the next phase. NASA is targeting a launch for Artemis III in 2027, followed by missions to the Moon's surface starting in 2028.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a successful Artemis II mission, demonstrating NASA's readiness for future crewed lunar exploration and Mars missions. It highlights significant technological achievements in spacecraft heat shield performance, rocket trajectory precision, and ground system resilience. The story has broad significance for space exploration and humanity's long-term space ambitions, with detailed context and clear positive impact.
articles.personality_title: NASA's Artemis II mission shows strong progress toward returning humans to the Moon
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – NASA is working on the Artemis program, which aims to send astronauts back to the Moon and eventually to Mars. Artemis II was the first mission with a crew to test important systems needed for these deep space journeys.

**What happened** – On April 10, 2024, the Artemis II spacecraft, called Orion, safely returned to Earth after flying nearly 700,000 miles around the Moon. It reentered Earth’s atmosphere at very high speed, protected by a special heat shield that kept the crew safe. The rocket that launched Orion, called the Space Launch System (SLS), performed exactly as planned, and the launch pad withstood the powerful liftoff with only minor wear.

**Impact** – This mission proved that NASA’s new spacecraft and rocket systems work well in real space conditions. The heat shield showed less damage than before, and Orion landed very close to its target spot. The launch pad improvements also held up, showing that NASA is ready for more challenging missions. These successes are important because they build confidence for sending astronauts to the Moon’s surface and beyond.

**What's next step** – Engineers will continue to study all parts of the spacecraft and rocket to fix small problems and prepare for Artemis III, planned for 2027. Artemis III aims to land astronauts on the Moon for the first time since 1972. The data from Artemis II will help make that mission safer and more reliable.

**One-sentence takeaway** – NASA’s Artemis II mission successfully tested key spaceflight systems, paving the way for humans to return to the Moon within a few years.
articles.personality_title_fr: La mission Artemis II de la NASA marque un pas important vers le retour des humains sur la Lune
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La NASA travaille sur le programme Artemis, qui vise à renvoyer des astronautes sur la Lune puis vers Mars. Artemis II était la première mission habitée pour tester les systèmes essentiels à ces voyages dans l’espace profond.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Le 10 avril 2024, le vaisseau spatial Artemis II, appelé Orion, est revenu sur Terre en toute sécurité après avoir parcouru près de 700 000 miles autour de la Lune. Il est rentré dans l’atmosphère terrestre à très haute vitesse, protégé par un bouclier thermique spécial. La fusée qui a lancé Orion, nommée Space Launch System (SLS), a parfaitement fonctionné, et la rampe de lancement a résisté au décollage puissant avec seulement de légers dommages.

**Impact** – Cette mission a démontré que les nouveaux systèmes de la NASA fonctionnent bien dans les conditions réelles de l’espace. Le bouclier thermique a montré moins de dégâts qu’auparavant, et Orion a atterri très près de sa zone prévue. Les améliorations de la rampe de lancement ont aussi tenu bon, montrant que la NASA est prête pour des missions plus difficiles. Ces succès renforcent la confiance pour envoyer des astronautes sur la surface lunaire et au-delà.

**Prochaine étape** – Les ingénieurs vont continuer à étudier toutes les parties du vaisseau et de la fusée pour résoudre de petits problèmes et préparer Artemis III, prévue pour 2027. Artemis III vise à poser des astronautes sur la Lune pour la première fois depuis 1972. Les données d’Artemis II aideront à rendre cette mission plus sûre et fiable.

**Résumé en une phrase** – La mission Artemis II de la NASA a testé avec succès des systèmes clés du vol spatial, ouvrant la voie au retour des humains sur la Lune dans quelques années.
articles.personality_title_es: La misión Artemis II de la NASA muestra un gran avance para regresar humanos a la Luna
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La NASA está trabajando en el programa Artemis, que busca enviar astronautas de nuevo a la Luna y, eventualmente, a Marte. Artemis II fue la primera misión tripulada para probar los sistemas importantes necesarios para estos viajes espaciales largos.

**Qué pasó** – El 10 de abril de 2024, la nave Artemis II, llamada Orion, regresó a la Tierra de forma segura después de volar casi 700,000 millas alrededor de la Luna. Reingresó a la atmósfera terrestre a muy alta velocidad, protegida por un escudo térmico especial que mantuvo a la tripulación segura. El cohete que lanzó a Orion, llamado Sistema de Lanzamiento Espacial (SLS), funcionó exactamente como se esperaba, y la plataforma de lanzamiento resistió el despegue con solo daños menores.

**Impacto** – Esta misión demostró que los nuevos sistemas de la NASA funcionan bien en condiciones reales del espacio. El escudo térmico mostró menos daños que antes, y Orion aterrizó muy cerca del lugar previsto. Las mejoras en la plataforma de lanzamiento también resistieron, mostrando que la NASA está lista para misiones más desafiantes. Estos logros son importantes porque aumentan la confianza para enviar astronautas a la superficie lunar y más allá.

**Próximo paso** – Los ingenieros continuarán estudiando todas las partes de la nave y el cohete para corregir problemas menores y preparar Artemis III, planeada para 2027. Artemis III tiene como objetivo llevar astronautas a la Luna por primera vez desde 1972. Los datos de Artemis II ayudarán a que esa misión sea más segura y confiable.

**Resumen en una frase** – La misión Artemis II de la NASA probó con éxito sistemas clave, allanando el camino para que los humanos regresen a la Luna en pocos años.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a sleek spacecraft shaped like a shining crescent orbiting a softly glowing full Moon above a calm ocean, with gentle ripples symbolizing splashdown near a distant silhouetted ship, all under a serene twilight sky blending soft blues and muted oranges.

articles.title: NASA shuts down 49-year-old Voyager 1 instrument to keep it alive
articles.summary: Voyager 1 just powered down a nearly 50-year-old instrument to stay alive in deep space. The spacecraft is running critically low on energy, forcing NASA to make careful sacrifices to keep its mission going. Despite the shutdown, it continues to send back unique data from beyond our solar system. Engineers are now working on a bold plan that could extend its life — and possibly revive the instrument later.
articles.content:
On April 17, engineers at NASA's Jet Propulsion Laboratory (JPL) in Southern California sent a command to turn off an instrument on Voyager 1 known as the Low-energy Charged Particles experiment, or LECP. The spacecraft, powered by a nuclear energy source, is gradually losing power, and shutting down this instrument is the most effective way to extend the life of humanity's first mission into interstellar space.

The LECP has operated almost continuously since Voyager 1 launched in 1977, nearly 49 years ago. It studies low-energy charged particles such as ions, electrons, and cosmic rays that come from both our solar system and the wider galaxy. Over the decades, it has delivered valuable insights into the interstellar medium, including detecting pressure waves and changes in particle density beyond the heliosphere. Voyager 1 and Voyager 2 remain the only spacecraft far enough from Earth to collect this kind of data.

Power Supply Shrinks After Decades in Space

Voyager 1, like its twin Voyager 2, is powered by a radioisotope thermoelectric generator that converts heat from decaying plutonium into electricity. Each spacecraft loses about 4 watts of power every year. After nearly 50 years in operation, available power has become extremely limited. Engineers must carefully reduce energy use by shutting off heaters and instruments while also preventing the spacecraft from becoming so cold that its fuel lines freeze.

During a planned roll maneuver on Feb. 27, Voyager 1 experienced an unexpected drop in power. Engineers realized that any further decline could activate the spacecraft's undervoltage fault protection system. This system would automatically shut down components to protect the probe, requiring a complex recovery process that carries additional risks.

The team decided to act before that could happen.

"While shutting down a science instrument is not anybody's preference, it is the best option available," said Kareem Badaruddin, Voyager mission manager at JPL. "Voyager 1 still has two remaining operating science instruments -- one that listens to plasma waves and one that measures magnetic fields. They are still working great, sending back data from a region of space no other human-made craft has ever explored. The team remains focused on keeping both Voyagers going for as long as possible."

Long-Planned Strategy for Instrument Shutdowns

The decision to turn off the LECP was not made suddenly. Years earlier, scientists and engineers developed a step-by-step plan for shutting down systems in a specific order while preserving as much scientific capability as possible. Each Voyager originally carried 10 sets of instruments, and seven have already been turned off. The LECP was next on the list for Voyager 1. The same instrument was shut down on Voyager 2 in March 2025.

Because Voyager 1 is now more than 15 billion miles (25 billion kilometers) from Earth, communication takes time. It takes about 23 hours for commands to reach the spacecraft, and once received, the shutdown process takes roughly three hours and 15 minutes to complete.

One small part of the LECP will continue operating. A motor that rotates the sensor to scan in all directions remains active because it uses very little power (0.5 watts). Keeping it on increases the possibility that the instrument could be restarted in the future if enough power becomes available.

Future Plans to Extend the Voyager Mission

Engineers expect that turning off the LECP will provide Voyager 1 with about one additional year of operation. During this time, the team is preparing a more advanced power-saving strategy known as "the Big Bang." This approach involves replacing several power-consuming components at once with more efficient alternatives to maintain enough warmth and functionality for continued science operations.

The Big Bang will first be tested on Voyager 2, which has slightly more available power and is closer to Earth, making it a lower-risk candidate. Tests are scheduled for May and June 2026. If successful, the same approach will be applied to Voyager 1 no earlier than July.

If the plan works as hoped, there is even a possibility that Voyager 1's LECP instrument could be turned back on in the future.
articles.reasoning: The article describes a significant and inspiring event in space exploration where NASA engineers are actively managing the aging Voyager 1 spacecraft to extend its mission life and continue gathering unique scientific data from interstellar space. The story highlights human ingenuity and long-term planning to preserve valuable scientific instruments, with potential future revival of a key instrument, which has broad significance for humanity's understanding of the cosmos.
articles.personality_title: NASA powers down Voyager 1 instrument to extend its mission in deep space
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context**  
Voyager 1 is a spacecraft launched by NASA in 1977 to explore space beyond our solar system. It uses a nuclear power source that slowly loses energy over time. After nearly 50 years in space, its power is running very low.

**What happened**  
On April 17, 2026, NASA engineers turned off one of Voyager 1's instruments called the Low-energy Charged Particles experiment (LECP). This instrument studies tiny particles from space but uses a lot of power. Shutting it down helps save energy so Voyager 1 can keep working longer. The spacecraft still has two other instruments sending back important data.

**Impact**  
Turning off the LECP means Voyager 1 can continue its mission for about one more year. This is important because Voyager 1 is the only spacecraft far enough to collect data from outside our solar system. The LECP has given scientists valuable information about space particles for decades, so careful planning was needed to decide when to turn it off.

**What's next step**  
NASA plans to test a new power-saving method called "the Big Bang" on Voyager 2 first. This method will replace some old parts with more efficient ones to save energy. If it works well, Voyager 1 will get the same upgrade. This could allow Voyager 1 to stay active longer and possibly turn the LECP instrument back on.

**One-sentence takeaway**  
NASA’s careful power management is helping Voyager 1 continue exploring deep space nearly 50 years after launch, with plans to extend its mission even further.
articles.personality_title_fr: La NASA éteint un instrument de Voyager 1 pour prolonger sa mission dans l’espace lointain
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte**  
Voyager 1 est une sonde lancée par la NASA en 1977 pour explorer l’espace au-delà de notre système solaire. Elle utilise une source d’énergie nucléaire qui perd lentement de la puissance avec le temps. Après presque 50 ans dans l’espace, son énergie est très faible.

**Ce qui s’est passé**  
Le 17 avril 2026, les ingénieurs de la NASA ont éteint un des instruments de Voyager 1 appelé l’expérience des particules chargées à basse énergie (LECP). Cet instrument étudie de petites particules venues de l’espace, mais consomme beaucoup d’énergie. L’éteindre permet d’économiser de la puissance pour que Voyager 1 puisse continuer à fonctionner plus longtemps. La sonde dispose encore de deux autres instruments qui envoient des données importantes.

**Impact**  
Éteindre le LECP permet à Voyager 1 de continuer sa mission environ un an de plus. C’est important car Voyager 1 est la seule sonde assez loin pour collecter des données en dehors de notre système solaire. Le LECP a fourni des informations précieuses sur les particules spatiales pendant des décennies, donc cette décision a été prise avec beaucoup de soin.

**Prochaine étape**  
La NASA prévoit de tester une nouvelle méthode d’économie d’énergie appelée « le Big Bang » d’abord sur Voyager 2. Cette méthode remplacera certaines pièces anciennes par des plus efficaces pour économiser de l’énergie. Si cela fonctionne bien, Voyager 1 aura la même mise à jour. Cela pourrait permettre à Voyager 1 de rester active plus longtemps et même de rallumer l’instrument LECP.

**Résumé en une phrase**  
La gestion prudente de l’énergie par la NASA permet à Voyager 1 de continuer à explorer l’espace lointain près de 50 ans après son lancement, avec des projets pour prolonger encore sa mission.
articles.personality_title_es: La NASA apaga un instrumento de Voyager 1 para extender su misión en el espacio profundo
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto**  
Voyager 1 es una nave espacial lanzada por la NASA en 1977 para explorar el espacio más allá de nuestro sistema solar. Usa una fuente de energía nuclear que pierde potencia lentamente con el tiempo. Después de casi 50 años en el espacio, su energía está muy baja.

**Qué pasó**  
El 17 de abril de 2026, los ingenieros de la NASA apagaron uno de los instrumentos de Voyager 1 llamado Experimento de Partículas Cargadas de Baja Energía (LECP). Este instrumento estudia partículas pequeñas del espacio pero consume mucha energía. Apagarlo ayuda a ahorrar energía para que Voyager 1 pueda seguir funcionando más tiempo. La nave aún tiene dos instrumentos que envían datos importantes.

**Impacto**  
Apagar el LECP permitirá que Voyager 1 continúe su misión aproximadamente un año más. Esto es importante porque Voyager 1 es la única nave lo suficientemente lejos para recolectar datos fuera de nuestro sistema solar. El LECP ha dado información valiosa sobre partículas espaciales durante décadas, por lo que la decisión fue tomada con cuidado.

**Próximo paso**  
La NASA planea probar un nuevo método de ahorro de energía llamado "el Big Bang" primero en Voyager 2. Este método reemplazará algunas piezas viejas por otras más eficientes para ahorrar energía. Si funciona bien, Voyager 1 recibirá la misma actualización. Esto podría permitir que Voyager 1 siga activa más tiempo y posiblemente vuelva a encender el instrumento LECP.

**Conclusión en una frase**  
La cuidadosa gestión de la energía de la NASA está ayudando a Voyager 1 a seguir explorando el espacio profundo casi 50 años después de su lanzamiento, con planes para extender aún más su misión.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a vintage spacecraft gently floating in the vast, softly glowing expanse of interstellar space, with one of its instruments symbolized by a softly dimming, intricate mechanical sensor slowly winding down while a small, delicate gear inside continues to turn, surrounded by subtle waves and particles representing cosmic rays and plasma, all rendered in natural, muted colors to evoke longevity, careful preservation, and hopeful endurance.

articles.title: Tiny frozen world unexpectedly appears to have an atmosphere
articles.summary: A 500-kilometre-wide object in a similar orbit to Pluto challenges our assumptions about small bodies in the outer solar system
articles.content:
A 500-kilometre-wide object in a similar orbit to Pluto challenges our assumptions about small bodies in the outer solar system

Artist’s impression of the trans-Neptunian object (612533) 2002 XV93 blocking the light from a distant star NAOJ/Ko Arimatsu

A small icy body as far away as Pluto has stunned scientists with the revelation that it has an atmosphere.

The object, located in the Kuiper Belt of distant frozen bodies at the edge of the solar system, is formally named (612533) 2002 XV 93 , after the date of its discovery nearly a quarter of a century ago. It has a diameter of less than 500 kilometres.

Advertisement

The object also belongs to a class of objects known as plutinos because they are in the same stable orbit as Pluto, completing three revolutions around the sun for every two made by Neptune.

On 10 January 2024, 2002 XV 93 passed in front of a distant star, causing what is called an occultation. Ko Arimatsu at Kyoto University and his colleagues observed this event from three locations in Japan.

If the body had no atmosphere, the star’s light would have disappeared and reappeared almost instantaneously when it went behind 2002 XV 93 .

Free newsletter Sign up to Launchpad Bring the galaxy to your inbox every month, with the latest space news, launches and astronomical occurrences from New Scientist’s Leah Crane. Sign up to newsletter

But instead, the team saw the star gradually fade and recover over about 1.5 seconds near the edge of the shadow.

“These gradual changes are best explained if the star’s light was bent by a very thin atmosphere around 2002 XV 93 ,” says Arimatsu.

The team estimates a surface pressure of about 100 to 200 nanobars, roughly 5 million to 10 million times thinner than Earth’s atmosphere and about 50 to 100 times thinner than Pluto’s tenuous atmosphere.

“You could not breathe it, feel wind from it, or see anything like Earth’s sky,” says Arimatsu. “But it is not negligible scientifically because even such a thin atmosphere can measurably bend starlight, and it tells us that volatile gases are present or being supplied around a very small icy body.”

The team couldn’t determine the composition of the atmosphere directly from the data. Arimatsu suggests methane, nitrogen and carbon monoxide are the most plausible candidates because they are among the few substances volatile enough to become gases at the very low temperatures of the outer solar system.

Another mystery is what has caused the atmosphere to form, with possibilities including volcanic activity, outgassing from the interior of 2002 XV 93 or even a cosmic collision.

“This discovery challenges our conventional view of small worlds in the outer solar system,” says Arimatsu. “Until now, clearly detectable atmospheres in the solar system were essentially associated with planets, dwarf planets and some large satellites. 2002 XV 93 appears to be one of the smallest solar system bodies yet with a clearly detected atmosphere.”

“There is an atmosphere, and we don’t understand why,” says Ben Montet at the University of New South Wales in Sydney, Australia.

“If you’re standing on the surface of this object, you’re not going to see a sky like [what] we have. But it certainly challenges the assumption that even a thin, transient atmosphere can’t exist on a body this small.”
articles.reasoning: The discovery of a thin atmosphere around a small Kuiper Belt object challenges existing scientific assumptions about small bodies in the outer solar system, representing a meaningful breakthrough in planetary science with broad implications for our understanding of solar system formation and evolution.
articles.personality_title: Small icy object near Pluto found to have a thin atmosphere
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Far beyond Neptune lies the Kuiper Belt, a region filled with icy objects. One of these, called (612533) 2002 XV93, is about 500 kilometres wide and orbits the sun in a similar path to Pluto.

**What happened** – On January 10, 2024, scientists in Japan watched 2002 XV93 pass in front of a distant star. Instead of the star’s light disappearing suddenly, it faded slowly and then returned slowly. This showed that 2002 XV93 has a very thin atmosphere bending the star’s light.

**Impact** – This is the smallest object known to have an atmosphere, even though it is extremely thin—much thinner than Pluto’s. It changes what scientists thought about small bodies in the outer solar system, showing that even tiny worlds can hold onto gases.

**What’s next step** – Researchers want to learn what gases make up this atmosphere and how it formed. They think it might come from gases like methane or nitrogen escaping from inside the object or from past collisions.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists discovered a surprisingly thin atmosphere around a small frozen object near Pluto, challenging old ideas about tiny worlds in space.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un petit objet glacé proche de Pluton possède une atmosphère fine
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Au-delà de Neptune se trouve la ceinture de Kuiper, une région remplie d’objets glacés. L’un d’eux, appelé (612533) 2002 XV93, mesure environ 500 kilomètres de large et suit une orbite similaire à celle de Pluton.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Le 10 janvier 2024, des scientifiques au Japon ont observé 2002 XV93 passer devant une étoile lointaine. Au lieu que la lumière de l’étoile disparaisse soudainement, elle s’est affaiblie lentement puis est revenue progressivement. Cela montre que 2002 XV93 possède une atmosphère très fine qui dévie la lumière de l’étoile.

**Impact** – C’est le plus petit objet connu à avoir une atmosphère, bien que très mince—beaucoup plus fine que celle de Pluton. Cette découverte change les idées des scientifiques sur les petits corps du système solaire extérieur, prouvant que même de petits mondes peuvent retenir des gaz.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs veulent déterminer quels gaz composent cette atmosphère et comment elle s’est formée. Ils pensent qu’elle pourrait venir de gaz comme le méthane ou l’azote s’échappant de l’intérieur de l’objet ou de collisions passées.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Des scientifiques ont découvert une atmosphère étonnamment fine autour d’un petit objet glacé proche de Pluton, remettant en question les anciennes idées sur les petits mondes dans l’espace.
articles.personality_title_es: Pequeño objeto helado cerca de Plutón tiene una atmósfera tenue
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Más allá de Neptuno está el cinturón de Kuiper, una zona con muchos objetos helados. Uno de ellos, llamado (612533) 2002 XV93, mide unos 500 kilómetros y orbita de forma parecida a Plutón.

**Qué pasó** – El 10 de enero de 2024, científicos en Japón observaron que 2002 XV93 pasó frente a una estrella lejana. En vez de que la luz de la estrella desapareciera de golpe, se fue apagando y luego volvió lentamente. Esto mostró que 2002 XV93 tiene una atmósfera muy delgada que desvía la luz de la estrella.

**Impacto** – Es el objeto más pequeño conocido con una atmósfera, aunque muy fina, mucho más que la de Plutón. Esto cambia lo que los científicos pensaban sobre los cuerpos pequeños en el sistema solar exterior, demostrando que hasta mundos pequeños pueden tener gases.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores quieren saber qué gases forman esta atmósfera y cómo se creó. Creen que puede venir de gases como metano o nitrógeno que salen del interior del objeto o de choques cósmicos.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Científicos descubrieron una atmósfera sorprendentemente delgada alrededor de un pequeño objeto helado cerca de Plutón, desafiando ideas antiguas sobre los mundos pequeños en el espacio.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a small, icy, distant dwarf planet with a faint, glowing halo of thin atmosphere bending the light of a distant star behind it, set against a dark starry background symbolizing the Kuiper Belt, emphasizing the mysterious and delicate nature of this tiny frozen world.
